fast.summary: A systematic review of NLP applications in computational advertising over the last decade, finding that information extraction and sentiment analysis dominate the field while advanced techniques like deep learning remain underutilized. The review highlights a gap between NLP research and modern mobile/app-based advertising formats.
fast.key_idea: NLP enables intelligent, real-time ad targeting by extracting meaning and sentiment from text, but the field has yet to fully leverage deep learning or adapt to mobile-first advertising environments.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners building ad targeting, brand safety, or contextual advertising systems can use this as a landscape map to identify underexplored techniques and avoid reinventing solved problems.
possible_use_cases: Contextual ad placement using real-time NLP analysis of webpage or app content
Sentiment-driven audience targeting that matches ad tone to user emotional state
Automated ad copy generation and optimization using large language models

deep.method_breakdown: This is a systematic literature review (SLR) that surveys journal articles, book chapters, and conference proceedings from the past ten years on NLP applications in advertising. The authors apply a structured search and selection methodology to identify, categorize, and synthesize findings across studies, classifying NLP techniques (e.g., information extraction, sentiment analysis, deep learning) and advertising domains (search engines, websites, job listings, mobile). Results are aggregated to map the current state, dominant approaches, and open challenges in the field.
deep.key_innovation: The novelty is in providing the first comprehensive, structured synthesis of NLP-in-advertising research as a standalone field. Prior work addressed NLP or advertising individually; this review maps the intersection, revealing a gap: the field lags behind in adopting advanced AI (deep learning, speech synthesis) and in addressing modern ad formats (mobile, in-app). The gap identification around automatic ad targeting as an unsolved problem is the primary contribution.
deep.technical_insight: The most important design choice is the dual-axis taxonomy — categorizing both NLP technique type and advertising medium. This reveals that the mismatch between technique maturity (older NLP methods dominate) and medium evolution (mobile/app ads are understudied) is the core structural problem in the field, which a single-axis review would miss.
deep.limitations: As a survey paper, it produces no new model or empirical result — its findings are bounded by the quality and coverage of the reviewed literature. The ten-year window and focus on English-language, indexed sources likely excludes significant non-English and grey-literature work. The categorization of NLP techniques is coarse and may not capture nuanced hybrid approaches. The rapidly accelerating pace of LLM-based advertising (post-GPT era) means this review was partially outdated at publication.
interview_questions: What gap in the literature motivated this systematic review, and how did you operationalize 'NLP in advertising' as a search scope?
Why do you think information extraction and sentiment analysis still dominate the advertising NLP literature despite the availability of more powerful techniques like transformers and LLMs?
The review finds that automatic ad targeting remains an unsolved challenge — what specific NLP subproblems make this hard, and what approaches seem most promising?
Mobile and app-based advertising are identified as understudied — what unique NLP challenges do these formats present compared to traditional search or web advertising?
How do you see the rise of large language models (GPT-4, Claude, etc.) changing the NLP-in-advertising landscape in the next five years, particularly around personalization and ad generation?
interview_answers: The review was motivated by the observation that programmatic/computational advertising had grown rapidly but no structured synthesis existed to map NLP contributions to it. The scope was operationalized through keyword-based database searches combining NLP technique terms with advertising domain terms, filtered by publication type, recency, and relevance criteria.

Legacy systems in ad tech are deeply entrenched — DSPs, SSPs, and ad exchanges were built around keyword matching and rule-based targeting, creating inertia. Additionally, labeled advertising datasets are proprietary, limiting academic access to train deep models. Sentiment analysis and IE are also directly actionable with interpretable outputs, which ad operations teams prefer over opaque deep models.

The hard subproblems include user intent disambiguation (the same query has different intent across users), cross-context relevance (matching ad creative semantics to page/app context), and real-time constraint satisfaction (inference must happen in under 100ms at auction time). Retrieval-augmented approaches and lightweight transformer distillation seem most promising for latency-constrained targeting.

Mobile ads face fragmented, noisy short-text contexts (push notifications, banner microcopy), multimodal signals (location, app category, sensor data), and stricter privacy constraints (ATT on iOS). NLP models trained on web-scale text transfer poorly to these sparse, context-dependent signals without domain-specific fine-tuning.

LLMs will shift the field from retrieval-based to generative ad personalization — dynamically generating ad copy tailored to user context rather than selecting from a fixed creative library. The main friction points will be brand safety (LLMs can hallucinate or generate off-brand content), latency (generation is slower than retrieval), and auditability (regulators will demand explainability for targeting decisions).
